text: This video illustrates a robot-assisted central hepatectomy indicated for centrally located hepatocellular carcinoma.
The patient was a seventy-year-old male diagnosed with a hepatic mass developed in the right lobe of the liver.
On this abdominal MRI, you can notice the direct invasion of the middle hepatic vein by the tumour mass, which is developed at the expense of segments four, five and eight.
The patient is positioned supine with a slight anti-Trendelenburg tilt on the operating table, preparing for the robot-assisted central hepatectomy.
Four 8mm robotic trocars are positioned along with two table-side assistant ports for the robot-assisted central hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses two bipolar forceps while the assistant controls the ultrasonic dissector, manipulating tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the round ligament of the liver, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the falciform and anterior coronary ligaments, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The dome of the liver is gently retracted downwards using the bipolar forceps of robotic arm number one.
The surgeon dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, using robotic instruments visible through the trochars and assistant ports.
A vessel loop is placed around the hepato-duodenal ligament, preparing for a potential Pringle maneuver during the parenchymal transection.
The surgeon employs the Glissonian approach for elective pedicle control, focusing on the hepatic pedicle structures.
The surgeon detaches the hilar plate from the quadrate lobe, noting that no branch originates from the anterior margin of its upper surface.
The surgeon controls the right anterior glissonian pedicle without performing liver dissection, utilizing the glissonian approach for elective pedicle control.
The surgeon makes a small parenchymal incision medially above the hilar plate, revealing underlying tissue structures.
The surgeon dissects the cystic plate, separating it from surrounding structures using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon identifies the right anterior glissonian pedicle located behind the cystic plate, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a laparoscopic bulldog clamp to the dissected right anterior glissonian pedicle for temporary inflow occlusion, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs intraoperative Doppler ultrasonography to confirm the effectiveness of anterior sector clamping, with the ultrasound images projected on the console screen.
The surgeon delineates the intersectoral plane along the margin of discoloration after temporarily clamping the right anterior septopedicle, highlighting the boundary between tissue sectors.
The surgeon delineates the medial plane between the left lobe and segment four, starting from the round ligament and extending to the origin of the middle hepatic vein.
The surgeon performs a transection using a caudal approach, following the path of the falciform ligament.
The surgeon dissects the segment four glissonian pedicle, encircles it with a vessel loop, and divides it using a haemlock clip.
The surgeon dissects the right anterior sector glissonian pedicle, controls it, and divides it using a vascular stapler, ensuring precise separation of the pedicle.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection along the medial plane, following the previously delineated intersectoral plane.
The surgeon encircles the origin of the middle hepatic vein, applies clips, and divides the vessel.
The surgeon identifies the origin of the right hepatic vein, visually distinguishing it from surrounding liver tissue.

The surgeon uses a caudal approach to transect the right intersectorial plane, initially following the delineated demarcation line on the surface, and then proceeding deeper along the trajectory of the right hepatic vein.
The surgeon approaches the most posterior margin, which remains to be transected, highlighting the challenge of reaching this area.
The surgeon uses a cotton tape to perform a hanging maneuver, facilitating the transection of the remaining posterior margin of the liver parenchyma.
The surgeon uses a cotton tape to define the posterior section plane, enhancing exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon extracts the specimen using an endobag through a midline incision, completing the removal process.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field following the completion of the hepatectomy, checking for hemostasis and biliostasis to ensure there is no bleeding or bile leakage.
The surgeon controls a bile leak originating from the right anterior Glissonian pedicle using interrupted non-resorbable sutures, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
A pathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of a sixty millimetre hepatocellular carcinoma.
The post-operative course was uneventful.
The abdominal drain was removed on day five after a CT scan and the patient was discharged on day eight.
